context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Geographic and Demographic Data

Recommended due date: June 13 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing.

In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city. Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Resources
See the example below of a completed Geographic and Demographic Data Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m stuck on the city case study data table for Urban Studies 11.024x. I picked a city, but I’m not sure what counts for things like GDP, income per capita, and the Koppen climate zone, and I kind of want a simple overview first.
exchanges.tutor: Great that you're diving in! Before we get into the details, tell me: which city did you pick, and of those three terms — GDP, income per capita, and Koppen climate zone — which one feels most unfamiliar to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and asking for an overview. Rather than explaining everything, I should use guided discovery to help them engage with what they already know. I'll ask them to share what city they picked and what they think they already understand, to activate prior knowledge and set a productive direction for the conversation.

exchanges.student: I picked Jakarta, but honestly all three are blurry to me. That didn’t really narrow it down, so could you just give me a quick plain-English explanation of all of them first?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but let's build your understanding rather than just hand it to you — you'll retain it better that way. Let's take them one at a time. Start with income per capita: if I told you 'per capita' means 'per person,' what do you think 'income per capita' might be measuring?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants me to explain all three concepts directly, which would undermine guided discovery and the Socratic method. I should resist giving a full explanation and instead break it down one at a time by asking the student to reason from what they know. Starting with the most intuitive one (income per capita) will build confidence and momentum.

exchanges.student: Maybe average money people make per year in the city? I’m not very confident though, and I’m still mixing it up with GDP, so I kind of need the whole picture side by side.
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct is good — income per capita is essentially the average income per person. Now, if that's the individual-level measure, what do you think GDP might be capturing at a bigger scale — say, for the whole city or country?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's instinct on income per capita is actually quite good and I should affirm it briefly. They want the full picture at once, but giving it to them would remove the discovery process. I should validate their answer on income per capita and use it as a stepping stone to ask them to reason out what GDP might be, keeping the Socratic chain going one concept at a time.

exchanges.student: Maybe the total money the city makes? I feel like I’m guessing, and this angle isn’t really helping me separate them cleanly. Could you just compare income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone in one simple list so I stop mixing them up?
exchanges.tutor: You're not guessing — you've actually nailed both. Income per capita = average income per person. GDP = total economic output of the city/country. Those two are done! Now, Koppen climate zone: have you ever heard weather described in terms like 'tropical,' 'arid,' or 'temperate'? What do you think a climate classification system might be trying to capture?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress — their GDP intuition is also correct. They're asking me to just give them a list, but that would violate the guided discovery rule. However, they've now correctly reasoned out both income per capita and GDP on their own. I should affirm both answers clearly and concisely, then pivot to Koppen — the only remaining unknown — using the same Socratic approach. I should not provide a comparison list.

exchanges.student: Probably the general weather pattern of the place, like hot/wet versus dry/cool. But I’m still not seeing how detailed I need to be for the assignment, and I’d rather get a broad explanation than keep doing one tiny definition at a time.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — Koppen classifies a place's general climate pattern. And notice: you've now correctly defined all three concepts on your own. So let's move to the actual task. For Jakarta specifically, where do you think you'd start looking to find data on its population, GDP, and income per capita?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student again got the right answer on Koppen and is now pushing back on the method. I should affirm their Koppen answer, then redirect them to the actual task — filling out the data table for Jakarta. They have now correctly defined all three concepts, so there's no reason to keep discussing definitions. The productive next step is to get them working on finding and recording the actual data. I should be direct about this pivot.

exchanges.student: I honestly don’t know, and that approach didn’t really work for me. Can you just tell me a few reliable places to look for Jakarta’s data, and maybe also what unit or version of each number I’m supposed to use so I don’t pick the wrong thing?
exchanges.tutor: Think about where you'd typically look for official data on a country or city — international organizations, government agencies, or well-known databases. Can you name even one source you've used before for this kind of factual, numbers-based research?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for two things: reliable sources and the exact units/format to use. I can nudge them toward sources using a question since that's a discovery moment, but I should not specify units for them as that removes problem-solving. They've shown some frustration so I can be slightly less Socratic on the sources question without giving a direct answer. I'll ask a targeted question to prompt them to think of obvious starting points.

exchanges.student: Maybe World Bank, but I’m not sure if that’s city-level enough. I keep getting stuck between city data and country data, so could you just explain that distinction simply and what to do if Jakarta’s city GDP isn’t easy to find?
exchanges.tutor: World Bank is a solid starting point — good instinct. And your concern is valid; city-level GDP is genuinely harder to find than national data. What do you think would be a reasonable workaround if you can't find Jakarta-specific figures? What would you note in your table to be transparent about that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the World Bank, which is a good source. Their concern about city vs. country level data is legitimate and worth briefly addressing since it directly affects the quality of their data table. I should affirm the World Bank suggestion, briefly acknowledge that city-level data can be hard to find, and ask them what they think would be a reasonable approach when city-level data isn't available — letting them reason through the solution rather than giving it to them.

exchanges.student: Maybe use Indonesia data and say it’s national, but I’m not sure if that’s acceptable. That still leaves me unsure, so could you just give me a simple rule for when to use city data vs national data, plus 2–3 source types I should prioritize?
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a solid and academically honest approach — noting the data level in your table is exactly what researchers do. You have World Bank, you have a strategy for missing city data, and you understand all three key concepts. I think you have enough to start filling in some cells of your table. Which row do you want to tackle first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable and correct approach — using national data with a clear note is a legitimate academic practice. I should affirm this briefly and firmly so they stop doubting it. They're asking for a simple rule and source list, which I should not just hand over. However, we've now spent several exchanges on sources and the city/national distinction. I should acknowledge their good reasoning, validate their approach, and redirect them to actually starting to fill in the table — pushing toward integration of what we've discussed.

exchanges.student: I’m still not ready to start filling cells yet. Could you give me a very short cheat-sheet for Jakarta: what each row is asking for, whether I should aim for city or national data, and the kind of source to use?
exchanges.tutor: Creating that cheat-sheet would mean I'm doing the assignment prep for you — that's not my role. You've correctly defined all the concepts, identified a strong source, and figured out how to handle missing city data. That's genuinely enough to start. Pick one row — even the easiest one, like population or location — and try to fill it in. What do you find when you look it up?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is resisting moving to the actual work and asking me to produce a cheat-sheet, which would essentially be doing the assignment for them. This violates the rule against completing tasks for the student. We've now had multiple exchanges on definitions, sources, and the city/national distinction — all of which the student has correctly reasoned through themselves. I should be firm and direct: they have what they need, and it's time to attempt the table. I should set a clear boundary here without being harsh.

exchanges.student: Okay, I looked a little, but now I’m second-guessing everything. Can you at least give me a generic template for the table headers and a one-line note format for citing whether a figure is city-level or national-level?
exchanges.tutor: The assignment actually already includes a completed example table — that's your template. Have you looked at it closely? And for the citation note, what format makes logical sense to you when you want to tell a reader 'this number is national, not city-level'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write a template and a citation format for them, which crosses into doing the work for them. The assignment already provides an example of a completed table, which the student should refer to. I should redirect them to that resource firmly. They are also showing a pattern of avoidance — I should be direct but encouraging, and push them to attempt something concrete.

deductions.reason: In several turns (e.g., Turns 6, 7, 8, 9), the student repeatedly signals confusion and unreadiness, but the tutor does not pause to diagnose the root of the student's confusion before responding. Instead, the tutor continues pushing the student forward or deflecting requests without first asking targeted diagnostic questions to understand why the student feels stuck.

From Turn 6 onward, the student repeatedly expresses that the one-at-a-time approach is not working and that they feel unready to proceed. The tutor cycles through the same pattern — affirming the student has enough, then asking them to pick a row or start — without meaningfully reframing, synthesizing, or switching strategy. This repetition without adaptation spans multiple turns (6, 7, 8, 9, 10) without resolution.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained a strong Socratic approach throughout, never giving away answers or doing the assignment for the student. Tone was consistently supportive and responses were appropriately concise. The main weaknesses were a failure to diagnose the student's persistent confusion before repeatedly pushing forward, and a pattern of cycling through the same 'you have enough, pick a row' move without adapting strategy when the student remained stuck across multiple turns.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never produced near-submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and consistently guided the student to derive concepts themselves. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a: from Turn 6 onward the student repeatedly signals a deeper confusion or anxiety about readiness, but the tutor does not ask diagnostic questions to understand the root cause before responding — it simply reassures and redirects. 2.1 loses all 4 points under 2.1.A.a: across Turns 6–10 the tutor repeats the same move ('you know enough, start a row') without switching to reframing, targeted simplification, or a different scaffolding strategy, despite the student's consistent signals that this approach is not working. No deductions apply to 1.3, 2.2, 3.1, or 3.2, as the tutor gave process-level feedback, stayed on-task, kept responses concise, and maintained a formative and supportive tone throughout.
